evidence_tier_definitions.peer_reviewed: Published in a peer-reviewed journal, verified PMID/DOI.
evidence_tier_definitions.clinic_presentation: Clinic document, slide deck, or non-peer-reviewed manuscript. Usable with caveat.
evidence_tier_definitions.clinical_experience: Clinician-attributed statement without a traceable document. Cite with explicit framing.
evidence_tier_definitions.organizational_primary: FEMM/RHRI institutional document (fact sheet, annual report, official website). Cite with document name and date.

curator_overrides.claim: FEMM was founded in 2012 and is headquartered at 228 East 71st Street, New York, NY 10021.
curator_overrides.body: FEMM (Fertility Education and Medical Management) is a women's health program founded in 2012 and headquartered in New York City. Its scientific foundation rests on decades of research in reproductive endocrinology, anchored in the work of James Brown, PhD (first to document the pattern of estrogen across a woman's cycle) and Dr. Emil Steinberger (hormonal framework for modern FEMM protocols).

source.citation: FEMM Health. Organization Fact Sheet. 2020.

curator_overrides.claim: As of 2020, the FEMM app had 400,000+ downloads across 161 countries and a 4.6 rating from 3,000+ reviews.
curator_overrides.body: Per the FEMM 2020 Organization Fact Sheet: app downloads exceed 400,000 across 161 countries; user rating 4.6/5 from more than 3,000 reviews; available on iOS and Android. Figures are point-in-time as of 2020 and should be refreshed from a newer FEMM institutional document before citing as current.

source.citation: FEMM Health. Organization Fact Sheet. 2020.

editorial_caveats: Time-anchor to 2020. Do not present as current without a newer FEMM source.

curator_overrides.claim: The FEMM app is available in 7 languages: English, Spanish, Portuguese, French, Hungarian, Croatian, and Swahili.
curator_overrides.body: The FEMM mobile application supports seven languages and runs on iOS and Android. Language coverage reflects FEMM's international reach, with providers and users across Europe, Latin America, Africa, and beyond.

source.citation: RRM Academy. FEMM pillar guide. 2026. (Vetted by Dr. Erin, FEMM-trained MD.)

curator_overrides.claim: FEMM is classified as a Sympto-Hormonal Method (SHM), combining cervical mucus observation with urinary LH testing.
curator_overrides.body: FEMM is a fertility awareness-based method (FABM) of the Sympto-Hormonal class. The biomarkers tracked are: (1) cervical mucus (daily observations of quality and quantity, reflecting estrogen and progesterone activity); (2) bleeding (menstruation patterns signaling prior-cycle hormonal status); (3) urinary LH testing (strips used from cycle day seven or first estrogenic mucus to capture the LH surge, which triggers ovulation within 24-36 hours).

source.citation: RRM Academy. FEMM pillar guide. 2026. (Vetted by Dr. Erin, FEMM-trained MD.)

curator_overrides.claim: FEMM uses a two-layer system: FEMM Teachers (non-prescribing chart educators) and FEMM Medical Providers (MD/NP/prescribers trained to diagnose and treat using RHRI protocols).
curator_overrides.body: FEMM Teachers are trained to help women observe and record biomarkers accurately, identify potential hormonal patterns, and refer clients to a FEMM-trained medical provider when abnormalities appear. Teachers do not prescribe and do not diagnose specific reproductive disorders. FEMM Medical Providers are clinicians (physicians, nurse practitioners, and other prescribers) trained through the FEMM Medical Management program to read patient charts alongside complete clinical workups, diagnose underlying hormonal conditions, and implement RHRI-developed treatment protocols. Certified providers must retake the Masterclass every two years to maintain certification.

source.citation: RRM Academy. FEMM pillar guide. 2026. (Vetted by Dr. Erin, FEMM-trained MD.)

curator_overrides.claim: FEMM Medical Provider certification requires completion of the Intro course (20 AAFP CME) and the Masterclass (18 AAFP CME), totaling 38 CME credits. Certified providers are enrolled as RHRI Fellows. Biennial Masterclass renewal is required to maintain certification.
curator_overrides.body: RHRI offers a two-step pathway to Certified FEMM Medical Provider status. The Intro course is online and self-paced, covering FEMM research foundations and reproductive physiology (20 AAFP CME). The Masterclass is offered live in-person and online annually, covering RHRI diagnostic and treatment protocols for ovulatory dysfunction, PCOS, thyroid imbalances, and infertility, with protocol textbooks included (18 AAFP CME). Clinicians who complete both steps (38 total CME credits) earn the Certified FEMM Medical Provider designation and enrollment as RHRI Fellows. Advanced topic courses (8-12 CME each) cover obesity, mental health, ovarian aging, and other subjects.

source.citation: RRM Academy. FEMM pillar guide. 2026. (Vetted by Dr. Erin, FEMM-trained MD.)

curator_overrides.claim: FEMM's clinical and research arm is the Reproductive Health Research Institute (RHRI), led by Dr. Pilar Vigil, MD, PhD, OB/GYN, FACOG.
curator_overrides.body: The Reproductive Health Research Institute (RHRI) is FEMM's clinical and research arm. It is led by Dr. Pilar Vigil, MD, PhD, OB/GYN, FACOG, a specialist in obstetrics and gynecology with a PhD in Biological Sciences from the Pontifical Catholic University of Chile. Dr. Vigil completed postdoctoral studies at the Texas Institute for Reproductive Medicine and Endocrinology and the Royal Women's Hospital in Melbourne, Australia, and is a faculty member at the Pontifical Catholic University of Chile. Her published research spans cervical mucus biology, reproductive endocrinology, and hormonal contraception, providing the scientific backbone for FEMM's clinical protocols. The RHRI is a multidisciplinary team of medical doctors, reproductive endocrinologists, neurobiologists, ecologists, nurses, and psychologists.

source.citation: RRM Academy. FEMM pillar guide. 2026. (Vetted by Dr. Erin, FEMM-trained MD.)

curator_overrides.claim: FEMM operates as a not-for-profit organization. FEMM does not share or sell user data with third parties and is not funded by drug or pharmaceutical companies.
curator_overrides.body: Per the FEMM 2020 Organization Fact Sheet: FEMM is a not-for-profit. User data is neither shared nor sold to third parties. FEMM is not funded by drug or pharmaceutical companies, which FEMM cites as ensuring freedom from commercial bias in its research and freedom from pressures to monetize user data.

source.citation: FEMM Health. Organization Fact Sheet. 2020.

curator_overrides.claim: FEMM's core clinical principle: ovulation is a vital sign. Disruptions to ovulation reflect underlying hormonal dysfunction, not normal variation to be managed with suppressive medication.
curator_overrides.body: A central tenet of FEMM's clinical model is that a woman's cycle is a continuous health record. Absent, irregular, or disrupted ovulation signals something diagnosable and treatable, rather than a variant of normal to be suppressed. This principle aligns FEMM with the broader RRM discipline: diagnose and treat the underlying condition rather than bypass or mask it.

source.citation: RRM Academy. FEMM pillar guide. 2026. (Vetted by Dr. Erin, FEMM-trained MD.)

curator_overrides.claim: Reproductive hormones affect whole-body health: brain function, bone density, blood sugar regulation, mood, and cardiovascular health. FEMM treats hormonal health as systemic health.
curator_overrides.body: FEMM extends reproductive endocrinology beyond the uterus. Estrogen, progesterone, FSH, and LH each influence cognition, skeletal integrity, glucose homeostasis, mood, and cardiovascular function. RHRI researchers have identified links between abnormal ovulation and conditions not typically framed as reproductive, including coronary heart disease and Type 2 diabetes linked to irregular cycles and chronic anovulation.

source.citation: RRM Academy. FEMM pillar guide. 2026. (Vetted by Dr. Erin, FEMM-trained MD.)

curator_overrides.claim: FEMM tracks four key reproductive hormones across the cycle: estrogen, progesterone, FSH, and LH.
curator_overrides.body: Estrogen, progesterone, follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH), and luteinizing hormone (LH) govern every phase of the menstrual cycle. Their interplay dictates follicle development, ovulation, and luteal-phase support. Disruptions in any of the four produce observable changes in the biomarkers (cervical mucus, bleeding patterns, LH strip results) a woman charts with FEMM.

source.citation: RRM Academy. FEMM pillar guide. 2026. (Vetted by Dr. Erin, FEMM-trained MD.)

curator_overrides.claim: Biomarker-to-hormone mapping: cervical mucus reflects estrogen/progesterone activity; bleeding qualities reflect prior-cycle hormonal status; urinary LH strips detect the LH surge that triggers ovulation within 24-36 hours.
curator_overrides.body: Each FEMM biomarker has an underlying hormonal basis. Cervical mucus quality and quantity changes daily in response to estrogen and progesterone; specific patterns (dry, moist, estrogenic, stretchy/lubricative) correspond to hormonal phases and signal the approach and confirmation of ovulation. Menstruation qualities (tail-end brown bleeding, premenstrual spotting, heavy flow) reflect the hormonal levels of the previous cycle. Urinary LH strips used from cycle day seven or from the appearance of estrogenic mucus detect the LH surge, which triggers ovulation within 24 to 36 hours, providing objective hormonal confirmation of the fertile window.

source.citation: RRM Academy. FEMM pillar guide. 2026. (Vetted by Dr. Erin, FEMM-trained MD.)

curator_overrides.claim: FEMM-trained providers anchor hormonal testing to the actual ovulatory event identified on the chart, not to a calendar-based day 21 progesterone draw.
curator_overrides.body: Standard reproductive workups often draw progesterone on cycle day 21, assuming ovulation on day 14 of a 28-day cycle. This model fails for many women. FEMM providers time bloodwork to the actual peak identified on the patient's chart, drawing serial hormone levels across the luteal phase to capture progesterone and estradiol curves that a single draw would miss entirely. This precision allows identification of luteal phase deficiency, ovulatory dysfunction, thyroid-related cycle disruption, and subtle estrogen imbalances that standard workups consistently miss.

source.citation: RRM Academy. FEMM pillar guide. 2026. (Vetted by Dr. Erin, FEMM-trained MD.)

curator_overrides.claim: FEMM uses a phenotype-based approach to PCOS, identifying whether the primary driver is insulin resistance, inflammation, adrenal androgen excess, or post-pill disruption, then targeting treatment to the specific phenotype.
curator_overrides.body: Rather than applying a one-size-fits-all suppressive protocol (e.g., oral contraceptives as default), FEMM-trained providers differentiate PCOS phenotypes and direct treatment to the underlying driver. Phenotypes include insulin-resistant PCOS, inflammatory PCOS, adrenal androgen excess, and post-pill (hypothalamic) disruption. Treatment paths differ accordingly, ranging from insulin-sensitizing agents and dietary intervention to anti-inflammatory protocols to hypothalamic-pituitary recovery support.

source.citation: RRM Academy. FEMM pillar guide. 2026. (Vetted by Dr. Erin, FEMM-trained MD.)

curator_overrides.claim: FEMM protocols address PCOS, endometriosis, ovulatory dysfunction, infertility, recurrent pregnancy loss, thyroid dysfunction, PMS/PMDD, depression, menopausal disorders, and more.
curator_overrides.body: FEMM's clinical scope spans reproductive and hormonal disorders that share an ovulatory or endocrine root. The RHRI develops diagnostic and treatment protocols for each condition, grounded in current biomedical research and updated as new evidence emerges.

source.citation: RRM Academy. FEMM pillar guide. 2026. (Vetted by Dr. Erin, FEMM-trained MD.)

curator_overrides.claim: FEMM is science-based and available to women of all faiths and none. FEMM has no religious affiliation.
curator_overrides.body: Unlike NaProTechnology, which was developed within a Catholic institution, FEMM has no religious affiliation. Its protocols are grounded in reproductive endocrinology and clinical research. Many secular health systems and independent providers worldwide use FEMM protocols. This distinction places FEMM explicitly within the Restorative Reproductive Medicine umbrella while differentiating it from NaPro, which predates the RRM framework by two decades and does not consider itself part of it.

source.citation: RRM Academy. FEMM pillar guide. 2026. (Vetted by Dr. Erin, FEMM-trained MD.)

curator_overrides.claim: James Brown, PhD, was the first to document and publish the pattern of estrogen across a woman's cycle and is often called the 'father of estrogen.'
curator_overrides.body: FEMM's scientific foundation rests on decades of research in reproductive endocrinology. James Brown, PhD, documented the estrogen curve across the menstrual cycle, establishing the hormonal baseline that modern FABM and RRM protocols build on. His work gave clinicians a framework for interpreting biomarker observations as hormonal events rather than as isolated physical findings.

source.citation: RRM Academy. FEMM pillar guide. 2026. (Vetted by Dr. Erin, FEMM-trained MD.)

curator_overrides.claim: Dr. Emil Steinberger's foundational endocrinology research helped establish the hormonal framework FEMM protocols use today.
curator_overrides.body: Alongside James Brown, Emil Steinberger's endocrinology research is foundational to FEMM's clinical model. His work contributed to the hormonal framework RHRI applies to diagnosis and treatment protocols, particularly those involving the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis.

source.citation: RRM Academy. FEMM pillar guide. 2026. (Vetted by Dr. Erin, FEMM-trained MD.)

curator_overrides.claim: RHRI publishes peer-reviewed research, develops CME-accredited (AAFP) courses, and collaborates with researchers across multiple countries and disciplines.
curator_overrides.body: The Reproductive Health Research Institute (RHRI) is a multidisciplinary team of medical doctors, reproductive endocrinologists, neurobiologists, ecologists, nurses, and psychologists. It publishes peer-reviewed research, develops CME-accredited courses through the American Academy of Family Physicians (AAFP), and collaborates internationally to expand the FEMM evidence base. Advanced topic courses (8 to 12 CME each) cover obesity, mental health, ovarian aging, and related subjects beyond the core Intro + Masterclass pathway.

source.citation: RRM Academy. FEMM pillar guide. 2026. (Vetted by Dr. Erin, FEMM-trained MD.)

curator_overrides.claim: FEMM-trained providers can diagnose underlying conditions after IVF failure that the IVF pathway never investigated. One cohort of 403 couples with an average of 2.1 prior failed IVF cycles achieved a life-table live birth rate of 32.1% through natural conception after RRM evaluation and treatment.
curator_overrides.body: When IVF fails, the underlying condition that prevented natural conception typically remains unaddressed. FEMM-trained providers perform diagnostic evaluations that often identify conditions the IVF workup never investigated, then design targeted treatment. The RRM evidence base documents this pathway: in a cohort of 403 couples with an average of 2.1 prior failed IVF cycles, RRM evaluation and treatment produced a life-table live birth rate of 32.1% through natural conception (Boyle 2018). Attribution: statistic is from NeoFertility/Boyle RRM practice; the pathway is applicable across FEMM Medical Management clinicians.

source.citation: Boyle PC, de Groot T, Andralojc KM, Parnell TA. Natural Procreative Technology for infertility and recurrent miscarriage: outcomes in a general practice setting. Ir Med J. 2018.

editorial_caveats: Statistic sourced from Boyle 2018 (NeoFertility/Irish GP cohort), not FEMM-specific. Framing: FEMM-trained providers operate in the same post-IVF-failure evaluation pattern documented across RRM.

curator_overrides.claim: FEMM is part of the family of fertility awareness-based methods (FABMs) and is specifically a Sympto-Hormonal Method (SHM), distinct from mucus-only (Billings, Creighton), sympto-thermal (STM), and rhythm-based methods.
curator_overrides.body: Within the FABM taxonomy, FEMM belongs to the Sympto-Hormonal class because it combines symptom-based charting (cervical mucus, bleeding) with urinary hormone testing (LH strips). This distinguishes FEMM from mucus-only methods like the Billings Ovulation Method and the Creighton Model FertilityCare System, from sympto-thermal methods that pair mucus with basal body temperature, and from calendar/rhythm methods. The addition of objective hormone confirmation is FEMM's technical differentiator.

source.citation: RRM Academy. FEMM pillar guide. 2026. (Vetted by Dr. Erin, FEMM-trained MD.)

facts.claim: In 90 nondiabetic subjects evaluated with both the pancreatic suppression test (PST) and OGTT, 37 (41.1%) were classified as insulin resistant and 53 (58.9%) as non-insulin-resistant.

source.title: A Simple and Improved Predictor of Insulin Resistance Extracted From the Oral  Glucose Tolerance Test: The I0*G60
source.authors: Contreras PH, Salgado AM, Bernal YA, Vigil P

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "37 (41.1%), were categorized as insulin resistant (IR subjects) by the PST, and the remaining 53 patients (58.9%)" Section: Materials and Methods.

facts.claim: The pancreatic suppression test (PST) has a correlation of 0.93 with hyperinsulinemic euglycemic clamp results, making it a clinically applicable alternative to the gold-standard clamp.

source.title: A Simple and Improved Predictor of Insulin Resistance Extracted From the Oral  Glucose Tolerance Test: The I0*G60
source.authors: Contreras PH, Salgado AM, Bernal YA, Vigil P

facts.claim: At fasting, mean insulin in IR subjects was 15.8 ± 1.8 mIU/mL versus 6.3 ± 0.5 mIU/mL in NIR subjects (P<0.0001); at 90 minutes, IR subjects peaked at 149.4 mIU/mL versus 48.6 in NIR.

source.title: A Simple and Improved Predictor of Insulin Resistance Extracted From the Oral  Glucose Tolerance Test: The I0*G60
source.authors: Contreras PH, Salgado AM, Bernal YA, Vigil P

source.authors: Vigil P, Meléndez J, Petkovic G, Del Río JP

facts.claim: Progesterone-only contraceptives disrupt normal ovulation and decrease estradiol levels, which may decrease insulin sensitivity and alter food intake and body weight in some women.
